title: Choice Specimens of American Literature
description: Choice Specimens of American Literature — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Choice Specimens of American Literature, and Literary Reader Being Selections fr" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: A Model of Christian Charity
memes.description: Winthrop's sermon delivered aboard the Arbella, articulating the Puritan vision of a "city upon a hill" and the covenant theology that shaped early New England.

memes.label: Abel Stevens
memes.description: American Methodist clergyman, editor, and historian; author of the definitive History of Methodism and a leading voice in nineteenth-century American religious scholarship.

memes.label: Abraham Lincoln
memes.description: Sixteenth President of the United States, whose speeches and writings are among the most enduring works of American political prose.

memes.label: Albert James Pickett
memes.description: American historian, author of the first comprehensive history of Alabama and an early chronicler of Creek Indian life.

memes.label: Albert Pike
memes.description: American poet, lawyer, Confederate general, and prominent Freemason; author of "Morals and Dogma."

memes.label: Alexander B. Meek
memes.description: Alabama poet, historian, and essayist, one of the few antebellum literary figures of Alabama, known for his writings on the French exiles of Marengo County and the Indian chief Weatherford.

memes.label: Alexander H. Stephens
memes.description: American politician and statesman; Vice President of the Confederate States of America and author of "A Constitutional View of the Late War Between the States."

memes.label: Alexander Hamilton
memes.description: American Founding Father, first Secretary of the Treasury, and principal author of the Federalist Papers; architect of the American financial system.

memes.label: Alexander Slidell Mackenzie
memes.description: American naval officer and author, known for his biographies of Stephen Decatur and John Paul Jones.

memes.label: Alexander Wilson
memes.description: Scottish-American poet and ornithologist, regarded as the father of American ornithology, author of the landmark American Ornithology with vivid prose descriptions of American birds.

memes.label: Alfred B. Street
memes.description: American poet and librarian; New York State Librarian known for nature poetry celebrating the Catskills and Adirondacks.

memes.label: Albert Taylor Bledsoe
memes.description: American philosopher, theologian, and Confederate official, author of works on moral philosophy and editor of the Southern Review.

memes.label: Alice Cary
memes.description: American poet and writer, known for Clovernook sketches and lyric poetry, elder sister of Phoebe Cary.

memes.label: Amelia B. Welby
memes.description: American lyric poet known for her sentimental verse, published under the name "Amelia."

memes.label: American Revolution
memes.description: The war for American independence (1775-1783) and the political revolution that established the United States, a defining subject in American literature.

memes.label: Anne C. Lynch Botta
memes.description: American poet, teacher, and influential literary salon hostess whose Fifth Avenue gatherings shaped mid-nineteenth-century New York literary life.

memes.label: The Autobiography of Benjamin Franklin
memes.description: Franklin's unfinished memoir, covering his rise from humble origins to international prominence, a classic of self-making and Enlightenment values in American literature.

memes.label: Benjamin Franklin
memes.description: Founding Father, printer, scientist, and diplomat, author of "Poor Richard's Almanack" and the "Autobiography."

memes.label: Benjamin Rush
memes.description: American physician, educator, and signer of the Declaration of Independence; a pioneering figure in American medicine and social reform.

memes.label: Benjamin Silliman
memes.description: American chemist, geologist, and educator, founder of the American Journal of Science, whose travel writings including A Tour to Canada exemplify early American scientific prose.

memes.label: Brantz Mayer
memes.description: American author, historian, and diplomat; founder of the Maryland Historical Society and secretary of the U.S. legation to Mexico, known for his writings on Mexican history, Central American archaeology, and the War of 1812.

memes.label: Cadwallader Colden
memes.description: Scottish-born American physician, natural scientist, historian, and colonial administrator; Lieutenant Governor of New York and author of the first systematic English-language account of the Iroquois Confederacy.

memes.label: Caroline M. Kirkland
memes.description: American writer and editor (1808–1864), pioneer of frontier realism in American fiction, author of A New Home—Who'll Follow? and Forest Life.

memes.label: Catharine M. Sedgwick
memes.description: American novelist of the early Republic, author of "Hope Leslie" and a pioneer of domestic fiction in the United States.

memes.label: Charles Brockden Brown
memes.description: First professional American novelist (1771–1810), pioneer of Gothic fiction in the United States, author of Wieland and Ormond.

memes.label: Charles Caldwell
memes.description: American physician, educator, and controversialist who founded the first medical school in Kentucky; prolific writer on medical and philosophical subjects.

memes.label: Charles Constantine Pise
memes.description: American Catholic priest, poet, and essayist; one of the first Catholic literary figures in the United States.

memes.label: Charles Dimitry
memes.description: Southern poet from a distinguished Louisiana family; his Civil War verse captured the Confederate soldier's experience with terse narrative force.

memes.label: Charles Gayarré
memes.description: Louisiana historian and jurist best known for his History of Louisiana, whose character portrait of Andrew Jackson appears in the anthology.

memes.label: Charles J. Ingersoll
memes.description: American lawyer, congressman, and political writer from Pennsylvania; author of a four-volume history of the War of 1812 and literary reminiscences.

memes.label: Charles Lanman
memes.description: American essayist, travel writer, and naturalist known for his nature essays and observations of the American landscape.

memes.label: Charles P. McIlvaine
memes.description: Episcopal bishop, chaplain of the United States Senate, and president of Kenyon College; author of sermons and theological works including "Attestations of the Resurrection."

memes.label: Charles Sprague
memes.description: American poet, known as the "Banker Poet of Boston"; author of popular occasional verse and satirical poetry.

memes.label: Charles Sumner
memes.description: American statesman, U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, and leading abolitionist orator; a founder of the Republican Party and architect of Reconstruction.

memes.label: Charles W. Upham
memes.description: American historian, Unitarian minister, and congressman from Massachusetts, author of Salem Witchcraft and history of Salem Village.

memes.label: Charles Wilkins Webber
memes.description: American naturalist, explorer, and journalist known for his writings on the American frontier and natural history of Wisconsin.

memes.label: Civic Duty in American Literature
memes.description: The concept of civic duty—the obligations of citizens to participate in public life, uphold the law, and contribute to the common good—as articulated in American literature from the colonial period through the 19th century.

memes.label: Cotton Mather
memes.description: American Puritan minister, theologian, and historian; author of the "Magnalia Christi Americana," the most comprehensive history of early New England.

memes.label: Daniel Drake
memes.description: American physician, medical educator, and author; a pioneering figure in medicine and scientific writing in the early West.

memes.label: Daniel Webster
memes.description: American statesman, lawyer, and orator; Secretary of State and one of the most celebrated speakers of the antebellum era.

memes.label: David Humphreys
memes.description: American Revolutionary War officer, aide-de-camp to General George Washington, diplomat, and poet of the Hartford Wits circle.

memes.label: David Ramsay
memes.description: American physician, historian, and statesman; delegate to the Continental Congress and author of the first comprehensive histories of the American Revolution written by an American participant.

memes.label: Donald G. Mitchell
memes.description: American essayist and novelist, best known under the pen name "Ik Marvel" for the sentimental classics "Reveries of a Bachelor" and "Dream Life."

memes.label: Edgar Allan Poe
memes.description: Poet, short story writer, and literary critic, known for "The Raven," "The Tell-Tale Heart," and his pioneering work in detective fiction.

memes.label: Edmund Ruffin
memes.description: American agricultural reformer, writer on soil chemistry, and ardent secessionist; editor of the Farmers' Register and author of "An Essay on Calcareous Manures."

memes.label: Education in American Literature
memes.description: The theme of education—encompassing formal schooling, self-cultivation, and the formation of American intellectual character—as reflected in American literature from the colonial era through the 19th century.

memes.label: Edward C. Pinkney
memes.description: American Romantic poet and naval officer from Baltimore; best known for the lyric "A Health," whose musical elegance and idealized portrait of womanhood made it one of the most admired short poems of the early American Romantic period.

memes.label: Edward Everett
memes.description: American statesman, orator, and educator; president of Harvard College, Governor of Massachusetts, and the most celebrated public speaker of his era.

memes.label: Edward Hitchcock
memes.description: American geologist, Congregational clergyman, and educator; professor of chemistry and natural history and later president of Amherst College, known for his efforts to reconcile geological science with evangelical Christianity.

memes.label: Edward Livingston
memes.description: American jurist, statesman, and legal reformer who authored the Louisiana Civil Code; served as U.S. Secretary of State under Andrew Jackson and as minister to France.

memes.label: Edward Payson
memes.description: American Congregational clergyman and theologian; pastor of the Second Church of Portland, Maine, known for his fervent piety and eloquent preaching style.

memes.label: Edwin P. Whipple
memes.description: American literary critic, essayist, and lecturer; a leading figure in nineteenth-century American literary criticism.

memes.label: Elisabeth F. Ellett
memes.description: American historian and writer, author of Pioneer Women of the West and chronicler of women's contributions to early American history.

memes.label: Ephraim C. Squier
memes.description: American archaeologist, diplomat, and ethnologist known for his pioneering studies of prehistoric earthworks and his diplomatic work in Central America.

memes.label: "Evangeline: A Tale of Acadie"
memes.description: Longfellow's epic poem of the Acadian deportation, following the heroine Evangeline's lifelong search for her lost love Gabriel.

memes.label: Evangeline Bellefontaine
memes.description: The Acadian heroine of Longfellow's "Evangeline," who spends her life searching for her lost love Gabriel.

memes.label: Evert A. Duyckinck
memes.description: American editor, literary critic, and essayist; co-editor of the influential literary journal Arcturus and a central figure in mid-nineteenth-century New York literary culture.

memes.label: Fisher Ames
memes.description: American Federalist politician, orator, and essayist; one of the most eloquent defenders of the Washington administration.

memes.label: Fitz-Greene Halleck
memes.description: American poet of the Knickerbocker Group, best known for "Marco Bozzaris" and the satirical poem "Fanny."

memes.label: Frances S. Osgood
memes.description: American poet and one of the most popular women writers of the antebellum period; known for her lyric verse, children's poetry, and literary friendships.

memes.label: Francis Hopkinson
memes.description: American Founding Father, signer of the Declaration of Independence, poet, and satirist best known for his humorous ballad "The Battle of the Kegs."

memes.label: Francis L. Hawks
memes.description: American Episcopal clergyman, historian, and editor; first president of the University of Louisiana and author of the "Narrative of the United States Expedition to Japan."

memes.label: Francis Parkman
memes.description: American historian and travel writer; author of "The Oregon Trail" and the multi-volume "France and England in North America"; master of narrative history.

memes.label: Francis S. Key
memes.description: American lawyer and poet; author of "The Star-Spangled Banner," the national anthem of the United States.

memes.label: Francis S. Osgood
memes.description: American lyric poet (1812–1850), known for her graceful occasional verse, literary salon in New York, and close friendship with Edgar Allan Poe.

memes.label: Francis Wayland
memes.description: American Baptist minister, moral philosopher, and president of Brown University; author of "The Elements of Moral Science," one of the most widely used ethics textbooks of the nineteenth century.

memes.label: Freedom of the Will
memes.description: Edwards's rigorous philosophical defense of theological determinism, arguing that human freedom is compatible with divine sovereignty.

memes.label: George D. Prentice
memes.description: American journalist and poet, longtime editor of the Louisville Journal; author of "The Mammoth Cave" and other poems.

memes.label: Geo. W. Bethune
memes.description: American Reformed Dutch minister, poet, and hymn writer; one of the most eloquent preachers of his generation and author of several volumes of devotional verse.

memes.label: George B. Cheever
memes.description: American clergyman, abolitionist, and author; a Congregational minister in New York City known for his sermons, his advocacy of temperance and abolition, and his influential religious writings.

memes.label: George Bancroft
memes.description: American historian and statesman; author of the monumental "History of the United States" and founder of the United States Naval Academy.

memes.label: George H. Calvert
memes.description: American poet, essayist, and literary critic known for his nature writing, his study of Coleridge, and his service as mayor of Newport, Rhode Island.

memes.label: George P. Morris
memes.description: American poet, journalist, and editor (1801–1864), co-founder of the New-York Mirror, author of "Woodman, Spare That Tree" and "Highlands of the Hudson."

memes.label: George Ticknor
memes.description: American literary scholar, Hispanist, and educator; author of the monumental "History of Spanish Literature" and a founder of the Boston Public Library.

memes.label: George W. Doane
memes.description: American Episcopal bishop, educator, and hymn writer; second Bishop of New Jersey and author of the enduring hymns "Softly Now the Light of Day" and "Fling Out the Banner."

memes.label: George Washington
memes.description: Commander of the Continental Army and first President of the United States, referenced in American literary selections as a figure of national leadership.

memes.label: George Washington Greene
memes.description: American historian and biographer; grandson of Revolutionary War General Nathanael Greene and author of "The Life of Nathanael Greene."

memes.label: Give Me Liberty, or Give Me Death
memes.description: Patrick Henry's address to the Second Virginia Convention, a defining expression of American revolutionary commitment and rhetorical power.

memes.label: Gouverneur Morris
memes.description: American Founding Father, diplomat, and senator who wrote the Preamble to the United States Constitution; served as minister plenipotentiary to France during the French Revolution.

memes.label: The Great Awakening
memes.description: A series of religious revivals that swept the American colonies in the 1730s-1740s, profoundly influencing American religious and literary culture.

memes.label: Greenfield Hill
memes.description: Dwight's long poem celebrating the Connecticut landscape and society, blending pastoral description with moral and patriotic themes.

memes.label: Gulian C. Verplanck
memes.description: American lawyer, politician, and literary figure, known for his essays on education, literature, and public affairs.

memes.label: Harriet Beecher Stowe
memes.description: Abolitionist author of "Uncle Tom's Cabin" (1852), the most influential American novel of the 19th century.

memes.label: Henry C. Carey
memes.description: American economist and publisher who advocated for protective tariffs and scientific agriculture, known as the father of the American School of economics.

memes.label: Henry Clay
memes.description: American statesman and orator; Speaker of the House, Secretary of State, and architect of the "American System" of economic development.

memes.label: Henry David Thoreau
memes.description: American naturalist, essayist, poet, and philosopher; leading figure of Transcendentalism and author of "Walden."

memes.label: Henry Lee
memes.description: American cavalry officer in the Revolutionary War, politician, and writer; father of Robert E. Lee, known as "Light-Horse Harry" for his daring cavalry exploits.

memes.label: Henry M. Brackenridge
memes.description: American writer, judge, and congressman; author of works on the early American frontier, Louisiana, and the War of 1812.

memes.label: Henry R. Schoolcraft
memes.description: American geographer, geologist, and ethnologist; pioneering scholar of Native American cultures and discoverer of the source of the Mississippi River.

memes.label: Henry Reed
memes.description: American educator, literary scholar, and translator; professor of English literature at the University of Pennsylvania and a devoted disciple of William Wordsworth, whose poems he edited for American readers.

memes.label: Henry Timrod
memes.description: American poet of the Civil War era, known as "the Poet Laureate of the Confederacy"; author of "The Unknown Dead" and nature poems.

memes.label: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
memes.description: The most popular American poet of the 19th century, author of "Evangeline," "The Song of Hiawatha," and "Paul Revere's Ride."

memes.label: Henry Ward Beecher
memes.description: American Congregationalist clergyman, social reformer, and essayist; one of the most famous preachers of the nineteenth century and a prominent abolitionist.

memes.label: Herman Melville
memes.description: American novelist, short story writer, and poet; author of "Moby-Dick," one of the greatest novels in the English language.

memes.label: Hester Prynne
memes.description: The protagonist of Nathaniel Hawthorne's "The Scarlet Letter," a woman condemned for adultery in Puritan Boston who transforms her punishment into a life of dignity, strength, and quiet defiance.

memes.label: H.N. Hudson
memes.description: American Shakespeare scholar, editor, and Episcopal clergyman; author of annotated editions of Shakespeare's works.

memes.label: Horace B. Wallace
memes.description: American essayist, critic, and lawyer of Philadelphia; author of literary and art criticism including "Art an Emanation of the Affections."

memes.label: Horace Bushnell
memes.description: American Congregational minister and theologian; a major figure in the development of American liberal theology, known for his essays on Christian nurture, the atonement, and the nature of human influence.

memes.label: Horace Greeley
memes.description: Influential American newspaper editor, founder of the New-York Tribune, and political writer known for his reformist views.

memes.label: Horace Mann
memes.description: American educational reformer, politician, and advocate for free public education; first Secretary of the Massachusetts Board of Education and founder of the common school movement.

memes.label: H.T. Tuckerman
memes.description: American essayist, art critic, and poet; author of "The Optimist" and "New England Philosophy," and one of the earliest American writers to take art criticism seriously as a literary genre.

memes.label: Hugh S. Legaré
memes.description: American lawyer, jurist, and diplomat who served as U.S. Attorney General, known for his classical scholarship and writings on immigration and education.

memes.label: Independence in American Literature
memes.description: The concept of independence—encompassing personal autonomy, national sovereignty, and intellectual self-reliance—as articulated in American literature from the Revolutionary period through the 19th century.

memes.label: J. G. M. Ramsey
memes.description: American physician and historian, author of the pioneering "Annals of Tennessee," a foundational work of Tennessee and frontier history.

memes.label: James Fenimore Cooper
memes.description: American novelist best known for the Leatherstocking Tales, including "The Last of the Mohicans"; one of the first major American writers of fiction to achieve international recognition.

memes.label: James Hall
memes.description: American lawyer, judge, and writer of the early frontier; editor of the Illinois Monthly Magazine and author of "Statistics of the West" and frontier sketches.

memes.label: James Jackson Jarves
memes.description: American art critic, collector, and newspaper editor; pioneering writer on art theory and founder of Hawaii's first newspaper.

memes.label: James K. Paulding
memes.description: American writer, satirist, and public servant of the Knickerbocker Group; Secretary of the Navy and collaborator with Washington Irving on the Salmagundi papers.

memes.label: James Kent
memes.description: American jurist and legal scholar; Chancellor of New York and author of the landmark "Commentaries on American Law."

memes.label: James Madison
memes.description: American Founding Father, fourth President of the United States, and "Father of the Constitution"; principal architect of the Bill of Rights.

memes.label: James Parton
memes.description: American biographer and historian; author of influential biographies of Andrew Jackson, Benjamin Franklin, Thomas Jefferson, and Aaron Burr, and one of the first professional biographers in the United States.

memes.label: James Russell Lowell
memes.description: Poet, critic, and diplomat, a central figure among the Fireside Poets and first editor of The Atlantic Monthly.

memes.label: James W. Alexander
memes.description: American Presbyterian minister, theologian, and professor at Princeton Theological Seminary; son of Archibald Alexander and author of Discourses on Christian Faith and Practice.

memes.label: Jane T.L. Worthington
memes.description: American writer, author of Love Sketches, known for sentimental prose meditations on family and the passage of time.

memes.label: Jared Sparks
memes.description: American historian, educator, and editor; author of "The Life of General Stark" and editor of the writings of George Washington.

memes.label: James A. Hillhouse
memes.description: American poet, dramatist, and orator of the early Republic; author of the dramatic poem "Hadad" (1825).

memes.label: James G. Percival
memes.description: American poet, geologist, and linguist; a Yale-educated polymath known for The Coral Grove and his melancholy Romantic lyrics, who also served as state geologist of Connecticut and Wisconsin.

memes.label: Jeremy Belknap
memes.description: American clergyman and historian, founder of the Massachusetts Historical Society, author of The History of New Hampshire.

memes.label: Jesse Buel
memes.description: American agricultural reformer, journalist, and editor of The Cultivator; advocate for scientific farming methods and agricultural education.

memes.label: John G. C. Brainard
memes.description: American poet and lawyer of Connecticut; author of sentimental and patriotic verse including "Lines to the Connecticut River."

memes.label: John Adams
memes.description: American statesman, lawyer, and diplomat; second President of the United States and a leading advocate for American independence.

memes.label: John Armstrong
memes.description: American soldier, politician, and writer; officer in the Revolutionary army and the War of 1812, author of military and historical works.

memes.label: John Bachman
memes.description: American Lutheran minister, social reformer, and naturalist; co-author of "The Quadrupeds of North America" with John James Audubon.

memes.label: John Blair Linn
memes.description: American poet, clergyman, and author of "The Powers of Genius," an epic poem on literary creativity.

memes.label: John C. Calhoun
memes.description: American statesman, political theorist, and orator; seventh Vice President of the United States and leading advocate of states' rights and the concurrent majority.

memes.label: John Charles Frémont
memes.description: American explorer, military officer, and politician known as "The Pathfinder" for his expeditions across the American West and his role in the conquest of California.

memes.label: John Dickinson
memes.description: American lawyer, politician, and writer; known as the "Penman of the Revolution" for his influential essays opposing British taxation.

memes.label: John Esten Cooke
memes.description: Virginia novelist and historian; a prolific chronicler of the Old South, best known for his Virginia historical romances and Civil War biographies.

memes.label: John G. Saxe
memes.description: American poet and humorist known for his satirical verse and witty social commentary; one of the most popular American comic poets of the mid-nineteenth century.

memes.label: John Gilmary Shea
memes.description: American historian of Catholic missions and the premier nineteenth-century scholar of early Catholic history in the United States.

memes.label: John Gorham Palfrey
memes.description: American historian, Unitarian minister, and congressman; author of the "History of New England" and early editor of the North American Review.

memes.label: John Greenleaf Whittier
memes.description: American Quaker poet, abolitionist, and one of the Fireside Poets; known for his nature poetry, anti-slavery verse, and narrative poems of New England life.

memes.label: John Heckewelder
memes.description: Moravian missionary and ethnographer, author of influential narratives on the Delaware and Mohegan Indians.

memes.label: John Henry Hobart
memes.description: American Episcopal bishop, theologian, and author; third bishop of New York and a leading figure in the revitalization of the Episcopal Church in the early nineteenth century.

memes.label: John J. Audubon
memes.description: American naturalist, ornithologist, and artist; author of "The Birds of America," one of the greatest works of natural history and artistic achievement in American letters.

memes.label: John Jay
memes.description: American statesman, diplomat, and jurist; first Chief Justice of the United States and co-author of the Federalist Papers.

memes.label: John L. McConnell
memes.description: American author known for his sketches of frontier life and western characters.

memes.label: John L. Stephens
memes.description: American explorer, travel writer, and diplomat; pioneered the archaeological discovery of the Maya civilization in Central America.

memes.label: John Lothrop Motley
memes.description: American historian and diplomat; author of "The Rise of the Dutch Republic," a landmark of nineteenth-century historical writing praised for its narrative power and exhaustive scholarship.

memes.label: John M. Harney
memes.description: American poet and journalist, one of the earliest literary figures of the American West; author of "Crystallina: a Fairy Tale."

memes.label: John M. Mason
memes.description: American Presbyterian clergyman and theologian; president of Dickinson College and a leading voice in American evangelical Protestantism noted for his controversial acuteness and religious eloquence.

memes.label: John Marshall
memes.description: American jurist and fourth Chief Justice of the United States; author of the landmark "Life of Washington" and the principal architect of American constitutional law.

memes.label: John McClintock
memes.description: American Methodist clergyman, theologian, and educator; distinguished for eloquence and learning.

memes.label: John Neal
memes.description: American author, critic, and editor; an early champion of American literary nationalism whose novels and critical writings helped shape the discourse of antebellum American letters.

memes.label: John P. Durbin
memes.description: American Methodist clergyman, educator, and travel writer; president of Dickinson College and one of the most eloquent divines in the Methodist Episcopal Church.

memes.label: John Pendleton Kennedy
memes.description: American novelist, lawyer, and politician from Maryland; author of Swallow Barn, a pioneering novel of Southern plantation life, and Secretary of the Navy under President Fillmore.

memes.label: John Pierpont
memes.description: American poet, Unitarian minister, temperance reformer, and abolitionist; known for "The Airs of Palestine" and the popular school-recitation poem "The Pilgrim Fathers."

memes.label: John Quincy Adams
memes.description: American statesman, diplomat, and sixth President of the United States; post-presidential career in Congress as a leading opponent of slavery and defender of the right of petition.

memes.label: John R. Thompson
memes.description: American poet and editor of the Southern Literary Messenger; best known for the Civil War poem "Music in Camp."

memes.label: John Randolph
memes.description: American planter, politician, and orator; U.S. Congressman and Senator from Virginia, known as "John Randolph of Roanoke," a fierce champion of states' rights and limited government.

memes.label: John Russell Bartlett
memes.description: American ethnologist, linguist, and bibliographer; author of the "Dictionary of Americanisms" and United States Boundary Commissioner.

memes.label: John Rutledge
memes.description: American statesman, jurist, and orator; Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court and a signer of the Constitution.

memes.label: John Trumbull
memes.description: American satirical poet of the Revolutionary era; one of the Hartford Wits, best known for the mock-epic "McFingal."

memes.label: John W. Francis
memes.description: American physician, historian, and essayist; a prominent figure in the intellectual life of nineteenth-century New York City.

memes.label: John Winthrop
memes.description: First governor of the Massachusetts Bay Colony, author of "A Model of Christian Charity" and key figure in early American Puritan literature.

memes.label: John Woolman
memes.description: American Quaker minister, abolitionist, and spiritual writer; author of the "Journal," a classic of American spiritual autobiography and one of the earliest sustained arguments against slavery by an American writer.

memes.label: Jonathan Edwards
memes.description: Theologian and philosopher, a central figure of the Great Awakening, author of "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" and "Freedom of the Will."

memes.label: Joseph Dennie
memes.description: American essayist and editor; founder of The Port Folio, the leading literary magazine of the early republic, known for his "Lay Preacher" essays under the pen name Oliver Oldschool.

memes.label: Joseph Rodman Drake
memes.description: American Romantic poet of the Knickerbocker Group; best known for The Culprit Fay, a fairy poem set on the Hudson River, who died at the age of twenty-five.

memes.label: Joseph S. Buckminster
memes.description: American Unitarian clergyman, biblical scholar, and preacher; minister of the Brattle Street Church in Boston and a formative figure in the emergence of American Unitarianism.

memes.label: Joseph Story
memes.description: American jurist, Associate Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court, and Harvard Law School professor; author of the "Commentaries on the Constitution."

memes.label: Justice in American Literature
memes.description: The concept of justice—encompassing divine justice, legal equity, and social fairness—as articulated in American literature from the Puritan era through the 19th century.

memes.label: Laura M. H. Thurston
memes.description: American poet; author of verse on nature, domestic life, and the American landscape, who died at the age of thirty.

memes.label: Leaves of Grass
memes.description: Whitman's revolutionary poetry collection, celebrating the self, democracy, and the body in free verse that transformed American poetry.

memes.label: Leonard Bacon
memes.description: American Congregational clergyman, editor, and social reformer; pastor of Center Church in New Haven and a leading voice in the anti-slavery movement.

memes.label: Lewis Cass
memes.description: American military officer, politician, and diplomat; Governor of Michigan Territory, U.S. Secretary of War, Secretary of State, and Democratic presidential nominee.

memes.label: Lydia H. Sigourney
memes.description: American poet, essayist, and educator known as the "Sweet Singer of Hartford"; one of the most popular and prolific women writers of nineteenth-century America, author of over sixty books of poetry and prose.

memes.label: Liberty
memes.description: The foundational political and literary theme in American writing, encompassing religious liberty, political independence, natural rights, and the abolition of slavery.

relations.label: Give me liberty, or give me death!

memes.label: Lucy Hooper
memes.description: American poet who died at twenty-five; author of "The Death-Summons," published posthumously in a collection edited by John Keese.

memes.label: Lydia Maria Child
memes.description: American novelist, abolitionist, and women's rights activist known for her historical fiction, domestic manuals, and antislavery writings.

memes.label: Lyman Beecher
memes.description: American Presbyterian and Congregationalist clergyman, theologian, and reformer; president of Lane Theological Seminary and patriarch of the Beecher family.

memes.label: Margaret Fuller Ossoli
memes.description: American Transcendentalist, literary critic, and feminist writer (1810–1850), first female war correspondent and author of Woman in the Nineteenth Century.

memes.label: Margaret M. Davidson
memes.description: American poet who died at fourteen, known for precocious verse collected posthumously; sister of Lucretia Davidson.

memes.label: Maria Brooks
memes.description: American poet known for the epic "Zophiël; or, The Bride of Seven"; praised by Robert Southey as "Maria del Occidente."

memes.label: Maria J. McIntosh
memes.description: American novelist and essayist; author of domestic fiction and works on Southern life.

memes.label: Martin John Spaulding
memes.description: American Catholic prelate and church historian, Archbishop of Baltimore and author of early Kentucky Catholic history.

memes.label: Matthew F. Maury
memes.description: American oceanographer, naval officer, and author of "The Physical Geography of the Sea"; pioneer of modern oceanography.

memes.label: Nathaniel Emmons
memes.description: American Congregational clergyman and theologian of the "New Divinity" school; known for his lucid prose style, original theological thought, and sixty-year pastorate in Franklin, Massachusetts.

memes.label: Nathaniel Hawthorne
memes.description: American novelist and short story writer, a key figure of the American Renaissance, author of "The Scarlet Letter," "The House of the Seven Gables," and "Twice-Told Tales."

memes.label: Nathaniel Parker Willis
memes.description: American author, poet, and editor; a prominent literary journalist and travel writer of the antebellum era.

memes.label: Nathaniel W. Taylor
memes.description: American Congregational theologian and educator; professor of didactic theology at Yale College and a leading architect of the "New Haven theology," a revision of Calvinism emphasizing human moral agency.

memes.label: National Identity in American Literature
memes.description: The literary construction and contestation of American national identity—the idea of a distinctive American character and cultural mission—from the colonial era through the 19th century.

memes.label: Natural Rights in American Literature
memes.description: The doctrine of natural rights—that all persons possess inherent rights to life, liberty, and property by virtue of their humanity—as articulated and applied in American literature.

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: The foundational text of Transcendentalism, arguing that the natural world reveals spiritual truth through individual intuition rather than tradition or scripture.

memes.label: Nature in American Literature
memes.description: A central theme in American literature from the colonial era through the nineteenth century, encompassing the wilderness, the pastoral, the sublime, and the spiritual significance of the natural world.

memes.label: New England
memes.description: The northeastern region of the United States, the cradle of American literature from Puritan sermons through Transcendentalist essays.

memes.label: Noah Porter
memes.description: American Congregational clergyman, philosopher, and educator; president of Yale College (1871–1886) and editor of Webster's Dictionary.

memes.label: Oliver Wendell Holmes
memes.description: Physician, poet, and essayist, author of "The Autocrat of the Breakfast-Table" and "Old Ironsides."

memes.label: Ormsby M. Mitchell
memes.description: American astronomer, educator, and Civil War general; founder of the Cincinnati Observatory and author of popular scientific lectures.

memes.label: Orestes A. Brownson
memes.description: American theologian, philosopher, and social critic who moved from Transcendentalism to Roman Catholicism, writing on religion, politics, and the moral duties of progress.

memes.label: Patrick Henry
memes.description: American revolutionary orator and Founding Father, whose "Give me liberty, or give me death!" speech (1775) galvanized the movement for American independence from Britain.

relations.label: Give me liberty, or give me death!

memes.label: Patriotism in American Literature
memes.description: The expression of patriotic sentiment—love of country, devotion to national ideals, and willingness to sacrifice for the common good—as a recurring theme in American literature.

memes.label: Peter S. Duponceau
memes.description: French-American jurist, linguist, and philologist who became a leading citizen of Philadelphia, known for his studies of Native American languages and his character sketch of William Penn.

memes.label: Philip Freneau
memes.description: American poet, journalist, and sea captain; known as the "Poet of the American Revolution" for his satirical and patriotic verse.

memes.label: Progress in American Literature
memes.description: The idea of progress—the belief in the steady improvement of human society in knowledge, morality, and material well-being—as a central theme in American literature from the colonial era through the 19th century.

memes.label: Providence in American Literature
memes.description: The doctrine of divine providence—the belief that God actively guides human affairs and history—as a pervasive theme in American literature from the colonial period onward.

memes.label: Ralph Waldo Emerson
memes.description: American essayist, poet, and philosopher who led the Transcendentalist movement of the mid-nineteenth century, author of "Nature," "Self-Reliance," and "The American Scholar."

memes.label: Religious Liberty in American Literature
memes.description: The principle of freedom of religious belief and practice, from Puritan "soul liberty" to constitutional disestablishment, as articulated in American literature from the 17th through 19th centuries.

memes.label: Richard Fuller
memes.description: American Baptist clergyman and orator; a founder of the Southern Baptist Convention and one of the most eloquent preachers of the antebellum South.

memes.label: Richard H. Dana
memes.description: American poet, essayist, and literary critic; a founder of the North American Review and an early voice of American Romanticism.

memes.label: Richard Henry Wilde
memes.description: American poet, lawyer, and Congressman from Georgia; remembered for his lyric poem "My Life is Like the Summer Rose."

memes.label: Rip Van Winkle
memes.description: Washington Irving's iconic character from "The Sketch Book," a good-natured Dutch-American villager who falls asleep in the Catskill Mountains and wakes twenty years later to find his world transformed by the American Revolution.

relations.label: The Sketch Book of Geoffrey Crayon, Gent.

memes.label: Robert C. Sands
memes.description: American poet and editor; author of "Weehawken" and other topographical verse, co-editor of The Talisman annual.

memes.label: Robert M. Bird
memes.description: American novelist, playwright, and physician; author of the frontier romance "Nick of the Woods" and the tragedy "The Gladiator."

memes.label: Robert Treat Paine
memes.description: American poet, editor, and Federalist satirist of the early Republic, best known for The Ruling Passion and the song "Adams and Liberty."

memes.label: Robert Walsh
memes.description: American journalist, essayist, and diplomat active in the early Republic, editor of the American Review and author of Didactics, Social, Literary, and Agricultural.

memes.label: Roger Williams
memes.description: English-born American Puritan minister, theologian, and founder of Rhode Island; a pioneering advocate for religious liberty and the separation of church and state.

memes.label: Rufus Choate
memes.description: American lawyer, orator, and statesman; U.S. Representative and Senator from Massachusetts, renowned as one of the greatest trial lawyers of the antebellum era.

memes.label: Samuel Davies
memes.description: American Presbyterian minister, educator, and fourth president of the College of New Jersey (Princeton); one of the foremost preachers of the Great Awakening in the southern colonies.

memes.label: Sarah A. Dorsey
memes.description: Mississippi novelist and memoirist whose fiction of plantation life and Civil War loss gave voice to the Southern woman's experience of defeat and displacement.

memes.label: Front Matter — Choice Specimens of American Literature
memes.description: Front Matter — Choice Specimens of American Literature

memes.description: J.G.M. Ramsey. About 1800- — 134. The Military Services of General Sevier.

memes.label: Slavery in American Literature
memes.description: The institution of slavery and the movement for its abolition, as represented and contested in American literature from the colonial era through the Civil War.

memes.label: Stephen Elliott
memes.description: American botanist, naturalist, and essayist of the antebellum South, author of A Sketch of the Botany of South-Carolina and Georgia and Views of Nature.

memes.label: Thanatopsis
memes.description: Bryant's masterful meditation on death and nature, written when he was only 17, establishing him as America's first significant Romantic poet.

memes.label: The Autocrat of the Breakfast-Table
memes.description: Holmes's celebrated collection of conversational essays blending wit, wisdom, and observation, originally published serially in The Atlantic Monthly.

memes.label: The Biglow Papers
memes.description: Lowell's satirical poems employing Yankee dialect to critique the Mexican-American War and the expansion of slavery.

memes.label: The Raven
memes.description: Poe's most famous poem, a narrative of mysterious visitation and psychological descent, celebrated for its musicality, stylized language, and supernatural atmosphere.

memes.label: The Scarlet Letter
memes.description: Nathaniel Hawthorne's masterpiece of American Romanticism, set in Puritan Boston, exploring sin, guilt, and redemption through the story of Hester Prynne.

memes.label: The Sketch Book of Geoffrey Crayon, Gent.
memes.description: A collection of 34 essays and short stories, including the immortal tales "Rip Van Winkle" and "The Legend of Sleepy Hollow," which established Irving as the first American writer to achieve international fame.
aliases: The Sketch Book of Geoffrey Crayon

memes.label: Theodore Dwight Woolsey
memes.description: American classicist, president of Yale College, and scholar of international law; author of "Introduction to the Study of International Law."

memes.label: Theodore Parker
memes.description: American Unitarian minister, Transcendentalist, and social reformer, a leading abolitionist orator and author of Lessons from the World of Nature.

memes.label: Thomas Buchanan Read
memes.description: American poet and painter; author of "Sheridan's Ride" and other patriotic and pastoral verse.

memes.label: Thomas C. Upham
memes.description: American philosopher, psychologist, and poet; professor at Bowdoin College and author of works on mental philosophy.

memes.label: Thomas H. Benton
memes.description: American politician and writer; U.S. Senator from Missouri for thirty years, champion of westward expansion and hard-money currency.

memes.label: Thomas Jefferson
memes.description: American statesman, philosopher, and architect; third President of the United States and principal author of the Declaration of Independence.

memes.label: Thomas S. Grimke
memes.description: American lawyer, social reformer, and essayist of the antebellum South, known for his advocacy of educational reform, temperance, and peace, and for his Addresses, Scientific and Literary.

memes.label: Thomas Starr King
memes.description: American Unitarian minister, orator, and author; known for his patriotic addresses and nature-infused religious essays.

memes.label: Thomas Wentworth Higginson
memes.description: American Unitarian minister, abolitionist, Civil War colonel, essayist, and mentor to Emily Dickinson; a prolific contributor to The Atlantic Monthly.

memes.label: Timothy Dwight
memes.description: Congregationalist minister, theologian, and poet, president of Yale College, a leading figure among the Connecticut Wits.

memes.label: Timothy Flint
memes.description: American clergyman, missionary, and writer; early chronicler of the Mississippi Valley frontier, known for Recollections of the Last Ten Years and his vivid descriptions of western boatmen and frontier life.

memes.label: Uncle Tom
memes.description: The devout enslaved man at the center of Stowe's "Uncle Tom's Cabin," whose Christian forbearance made him one of the most consequential characters in American literary history.

memes.label: Uncle Tom's Cabin
memes.description: Harriet Beecher Stowe's landmark antislavery novel that galvanized Northern opposition to slavery and became the best-selling American novel of the nineteenth century.

memes.label: Virginia
memes.description: The oldest English colony in America and the birthplace of Revolutionary oratory, represented in the anthology by Patrick Henry.

memes.label: Virtue in American Literature
memes.description: The concept of virtue—encompassing personal morality, civic character, and public spirit—as a central theme in American literature from the Puritan era through the early Republic.

memes.label: Walt Whitman
memes.description: Poet of democracy and the self, author of "Leaves of Grass" (1855), a revolutionary work in American poetry.

memes.label: Washington Allston
memes.description: American painter and poet, a central figure in early American Romanticism, known for his historical and biblical paintings and his novel "Monaldi".

memes.label: Washington Irving
memes.description: America's first internationally recognized literary figure, author of "The Sketch Book," "Rip Van Winkle," and "The Legend of Sleepy Hollow."

relations.label: The Sketch Book of Geoffrey Crayon, Gent.

memes.label: William Bartram
memes.description: American naturalist, botanist, and explorer; author of Travels through North and South Carolina, Georgia, East and West Florida, a foundational work of American nature writing celebrated for its lyrical descriptions of the southern wilderness.

memes.label: William Byrd
memes.description: Virginia planter, surveyor, and writer; author of the "History of the Dividing Line" and one of the most accomplished prose stylists of colonial America.

memes.label: William Clifton
memes.description: American poet of the early Republic; author of "To Fancy" and other lyric poems in the neoclassical tradition, published posthumously after his early death at age eighteen.

memes.label: William Cullen Bryant
memes.description: Poet and journalist, author of "Thanatopsis" and long-time editor of the New York Evening Post.

memes.label: William Dean Howells
memes.description: American novelist, literary critic, and editor of The Atlantic Monthly; leading figure of American literary realism and mentor to a generation of American writers.

memes.label: William Ellery Channing
memes.description: American Unitarian minister, theologian, and essayist; leading figure of the liberal religious movement and influence on Transcendentalism.

memes.label: William Gaston
memes.description: American jurist, politician, and moral essayist from North Carolina; author of "The Importance of Integrity," a widely cited discourse on honesty as the foundation of public and private virtue.

memes.label: William H. Prescott
memes.description: American historian renowned for his narrative histories of the Spanish conquest of Mexico and Peru.

memes.label: William H. Seward
memes.description: American statesman, Governor of New York, U.S. Senator, and Secretary of State under Abraham Lincoln; architect of the Alaska Purchase.

memes.label: William Pinkney
memes.description: American lawyer, orator, and diplomat; United States Attorney General and minister to Russia, renowned for his eloquence at the Supreme Court bar.

memes.label: William R. Williams
memes.description: American Baptist clergyman and theologian; pastor of the Amity Street Baptist Church in New York and author of Religious Progress and Lectures on the Lord's Prayer.

memes.label: William Smith
memes.description: American historian, jurist, and Anglican clergyman; Chief Justice of the Province of New York and author of the first comprehensive history of the colony.

memes.label: William Stith
memes.description: American historian, Anglican clergyman, and the third president of the College of William and Mary; author of the first comprehensive history of colonial Virginia.

memes.label: William Ware
memes.description: American Unitarian clergyman and novelist; author of Zenobia, or the Fall of Palmyra and Aurelian, or Rome in the Third Century — historical novels set in the early Christian era that enjoyed wide popularity in the nineteenth century.

memes.label: William Wirt
memes.description: American author, lawyer, and statesman; ninth U.S. Attorney General and biographer of Patrick Henry.

memes.label: Willis G. Clark
memes.description: American poet and journalist; editor of the Philadelphia Gazette and author of religious and occasional verse.

memes.label: William Gilmore Simms
memes.description: American novelist, poet, and historian, a leading literary figure of the antebellum South.

memes.label: Yale College
memes.description: The collegiate institution where Jonathan Edwards and Timothy Dwight studied and taught, a center of American theological and literary education.
